intro: Java's JVM ecosystem and object-oriented class hierarchy contrast with Go's lean compiled runtime and structural typing. Converting Java to Go replaces abstract classes and interfaces with Go interfaces (defined by method sets, not inheritance), maps Java's thread-pool executor to goroutines and channels, and substitutes Java's checked exceptions with Go's explicit `(value, error)` return convention. The result is typically leaner, faster, and more explicit.

diffs.from: Nominal typing; abstract classes, explicit implements, extends.
diffs.to: Structural typing; interfaces satisfied implicitly by method sets. No inheritance.

diffs.from: Checked exceptions declared with throws. try/catch/finally.
diffs.to: Errors as return values. No exceptions. defer for cleanup.
Goroutines + channels. Lightweight (4 KB stack). Scheduler built into runtime.

diffs.from: JVM heap with generational GC. Objects always on heap.
diffs.to: GC but compiler can stack-allocate via escape analysis. Smaller runtime footprint.

diffs.from: JVM bytecode; requires JRE. Fat JARs for distribution.
diffs.to: Single statically-linked binary. No runtime dependency.

examples.title: Interface implementation
examples.description: Java's explicit implements becomes implicit structural satisfaction in Go.

examples.title: Checked exception → error return
examples.description: Java throws IOException becomes Go's (T, error) return pattern with defer for cleanup.

tips.title: Replace class hierarchy with composition
tips.body: Java's extends becomes embedding: type Manager struct { Employee }. Go favours composition over inheritance — embed structs to reuse behaviour.

tips.title: throws → (T, error) returns
tips.body: Remove all throws declarations. Every function that can fail returns (value, error). Callers check if err != nil immediately after the call.

tips.title: ExecutorService → goroutines
tips.body: Java thread pools map to go func() {...}. Use sync.WaitGroup to wait for completion and buffered channels to limit concurrency.

tips.title: finally → defer
tips.body: Java's finally block (for cleanup) maps to defer in Go. defer f.Close() runs when the surrounding function returns, regardless of how it exits.
